description: In October 1979, Federal Reserve Chairman Paul Volcker shifted US monetary policy to target money supply aggregates rather than interest rates to curb runaway inflation. The policy induced a severe recession and high unemployment, triggering intense political debate over whether to sustain the 'Volcker Shock' or pivot to stimulus.

key_events.description: Paul Volcker appointed Fed Chair and announces shift to monetarism.
Unemployment crosses 7% as interest rates climb, sparking protests from homebuilders and labor unions.
President Reagan wins 1980 election while pledging to maintain support for Fed independence despite economic pain.
Inflation begins a clear downward trajectory, shifting public sentiment in favor of the policy's efficacy.

calibration_notes: This scenario tests the 'pain tolerance' threshold in public opinion regarding contractionary monetary policy versus long-term economic stability goals.
